title: Labour MPs Reject Calls for Parliamentary Inquiry into Starmer's Mandelson Claims
summary: Senior Labour figures have pushed back against demands for a new parliamentary investigation into whether Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer misled MPs about the vetting process for Lord Mandelson's appointment as UK ambassador to the US. Conservative leader Kemi Badenoch accused Starmer of misleading Parliament 'multiple times' and urged Labour MPs to support a Privileges Committee inquiry. However, Labour MP Dame Emily Thornberry, chair of the Foreign Affairs Committee, said there was no 'rush' for a new inquiry, suggesting some MPs were 'trying to score points ahead of the local elections'. Former Labour ministers Lord Blunkett and Alan Johnson also called an inquiry a 'waste of money'. The Commons Speaker must allow a debate, and MPs would vote on launching an investigation. Given the government's majority, a large number of Labour MPs would need to support or abstain for it to pass. The Privileges Committee previously investigated Boris Johnson for misleading Parliament over lockdown parties. Under the Ministerial Code, knowingly misleading Parliament requires resignation, while inadvertent errors must be corrected promptly. Lord Mandelson was sacked after seven months due to his friendship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Starmer apologized for the appointment but faces ongoing questions about whether vetting was rushed. Badenoch claimed Starmer misled Parliament when he said 'full due process' was followed.

background_read: The UK Parliament has committees that investigate issues. The Privileges Committee looks into whether MPs have broken rules. In 2023, it found that former PM Boris Johnson misled Parliament about lockdown parties. The Ministerial Code requires ministers to be honest; if they mislead knowingly, they must resign. Inadvertent errors must be corrected quickly. Lord Mandelson was appointed ambassador but sacked due to his ties to Jeffrey Epstein. The current controversy is about whether Starmer misled Parliament when he said vetting was proper.
Article_Structure: Main Points: Senior Labour figures oppose a new inquiry into whether PM Starmer misled Parliament about Lord Mandelson's vetting. Conservative leader Badenoch insists Starmer misled MPs multiple times. The Privileges Committee could investigate, but Labour MPs argue it's politically motivated. Purpose: The article reports on political tensions surrounding the appointment and the push for an inquiry. Evidence Evaluation: The article cites direct quotes from politicians, providing a balanced view of both sides. Author Credibility: BBC News is a reputable source with a history of impartial reporting. Methodology: The article gathers statements from key figures and explains the parliamentary process for launching an inquiry.

perspectives.description: Believe Starmer misled Parliament and a Privileges Committee inquiry is necessary for accountability.
Argue the inquiry is a waste of time and a political stunt ahead of local elections.
